title: Marketing Cartoon - Tom Fishburne
content: Marketing cartoon about AI and marketing (content unavailable - login wall)

title: AI Slop in Landing Pages - Sachin Rekhi
content: The latest example of AI slop: I'm seeing a ton of people launch landing pages built with AI prototyping tools and it's obvious as they all have the same mediocre structure and styling. Honestly folks would get more differentiated results by simply using the previous generation of no code tools like Wix and Squarespace because they at least offer different, but beautiful by default themes.
comments:
Richard Ewing: The problem isn't the AI prototyping tool; it's the lack of a Discovery Process. The tool is just amplifying the mediocrity of the prompt.

Albert Wang: It proves the point this is the age of Eval. The ability to tell what is great and engaging UX, and what is crap, becomes extra important. Without that 'taste,' a non-designer can vibecode in Lovable to create a 'safe' landing page.

Angus Berry: 100% agree...There is too much hype around look at what I built with no code at all using AI! If we don't actually know what we're doing, we're just handing agency over to an opaque machine.

Anne T Griffin: I can immediately spot design styles that are V0 or Lovable. Most people using them don't have design skills and it defaults to the same styles every time.

title: It's True - AI Copywriting Is Driving Your Customers Away
content: Whether you use Jasper AI, ChatGPT, or an AI blog writer that guarantees whatever you generate will sound authentic, 99.9% of the time, this is not the case. AI Copywriting Is Repetitive & Robotic. Phrases such as 'In the digital world', 'The benefits are top notch', and 'Let's delve into this' can all sound convincing but you start to see the holes forming. AI copywriting is a time-saving tool but not reliable for serious SEO strategy. Nobody wants to be 'sold' to. Copywriting is about appealing to emotions and connecting with readers on a deeper level. AI remains emotionless and incapable of understanding human emotions. AI-generated copy often comes across as sterile, detached, and unable to elicit the desired emotional response. AI Copy Is Lazy Marketing - most of the time, it's downright lazy. Poorly written AI content is extremely off-putting for your customers. It tells them that you just don't care. AI Hallucinations - If your industry is complex, niche, or unique, you will struggle to produce quality content using an AI copywriting tool.
comments: Asher Busingye: The section about AI being robotic and repetitive is so true. As AI is used by everyone under the sun, without the proper input it's bound to spit out the same copy. It just doesn't have that human touch.

title: What are your AI writing pet peeves? - Jon Miller
content: I'm a fan of using AI in my work. That said, I'm getting tired of the same phrases and structures showing up over and over. Here are 11 of the most overused AI words and phrases: 1. Anything that is rapidly evolving or ever-evolving. 2. Starting sentences with 'In today's digital age'. 3. Making something 'transformative' or 'crucial'. 4. Using 'Whether you are...'. 5. Reminding the reader to remember. 6. Telling us to 'think of X as'. 7. Using 'not only... but also'. 8. Saying something is 'all about'. 9. Repetitive paragraphs making the same point multiple times. 10. Overuse of 'delve', 'unlock', 'ensure'. 11. Ending with 'In conclusion'. Tips to sound human: imperfections are your friend, AI struggles with tricolon, AI doesn't use parentheses or em dashes, AI sentences have uniform length.
comments:
Karen Cooper: Here's to no more 'unlock' or 'transform' or 'boost'.

Mohan Kompella: 'In the realm of'

title: Human Copywriters: Don't Rely Solely on AI - Prerna Malik
content: So let me get this straight... We aren't using the following because AI has ruined it for us: em dashes, the word 'quietly', The 3-part structure, 'It's not this, it's that' structure, Anything else that we've seen more than 5 times on the internet. ALL of these have been used by us, human copywriters, for DECADES, which is why ironically, AI uses them. That doesn't mean you strip your copy of these. It means you edit like you always did, like a person who writes for people. Use AI. But use your superior brain more.
comments: Tania Strauss: The idea that something my mind has always done automatically is now considered an AI tell is wild to me!

title: Unraveling the Intricate Tapestry of AI's Meticulous Verbosity - Uli Hitzel
content: How is it possible that some of the best performing language models still struggle to communicate in a concise and coherent manner? Words like 'unravel', 'intricate', and 'delve into' are the best examples of verbose language that continuously creeps into AI-generated content. Those are often a clear sign that 'authors' have taken too many shortcuts and simply used ChatGPT to write an article. Part of the reason is shared training data - it's almost as if all the major language models went to the same school. It's nearly impossible to get it to suggest a title without a colon. Language models typically 'stream out tokens' predicting the next word without the ability to revise their response.
comments:
Stephanie Krishnan: I used 'unravel' and colons in titles BEFORE LLMs... One useful hack: 'remove superlatives' in prompts.

Ritika J.: Spot on! Love the 'AI finishing school' concept.

title: 'Delve' and 'Realm' are so ChatGPT - Alexandra Suarez-Carlo
content: 'Delve' and 'Realm' are so ChatGPT. Every time I see these words on a post, I lose interest. Be authentic. People can spot an impostor from miles away. Over-relying on Chat GPT could disconnect us from our human side. Speak from the Heart. Resorting to canned responses can suck the life out of our conversations, making them feel robotic and empty.
comments:
Jesabel Rivera-Guerra: I use chatGPT to brainstorm ideas, still use it as inspiration to make it human. Otherwise, chatGPT is just talking and responding to itself.

Caroline Brookfield: I use Chat GPT for outlines. I'm tired of 'unleash'.

Jeff Angus: I noticed 'delve' come up so much. You were ahead spotting this annoying trend.

Martyna Krol: Delve and 'embark on a journey' give me shivers. 'Seamlessly' and 'pivotal'.

title: AI Slop Flooding the Internet - Robleh Jama
content: I'm getting tired of all of the AI slop flooding the internet. Every enterprise software company is rebranding to 'AI-powered'. Glorified chatbots are now AI assistants. Gen AI tools are all pumping out the same recycled stuff. Writing is looking all cookie-cutter and soulless. This rush to add LLMs without any clear benefit is leading to the anti-AI trend.
comments:
Mohamed Jama: 'it is tempting, if the only tool you have is a hammer, to treat everything as if it were a nail.'

Mike Shoss: Most are just updating their marketing without the tech to check a box and ride the wave.

Edward Chik: There's a lot of slapping AI/LLM onto older products as a rebrand, it wasn't too long ago when 'blockchain' was being used this way.

title: AI Homogenization: Rutgers Study Reveals Limited Creative Possibilities - Noric Dilanchian
content: AI-induced cultural stagnation is no longer speculation - it's already happening. Evidence shows AI replication defaults to homogenisation. 'The experimental evidence shows that when generative models iterate on their own outputs, they do not explore the full space of creative possibilities but instead converge toward a limited set of high-probability attractors.' In the last century TV was similar - the default is slop.

title: AI Fatigue and Marketing BS: A Toxic Mix - Zach Tratar
content: A top trend right now: AI fatigue is combining with marketing BS -- a terrible combination! No one wants to use tools anymore. Product Hunt grew in 2015 because people actively wanted to try new products. We're at a different point of the technology adoption curve. Coding has commoditized, products are plentiful, marketing channels are saturated. Companies have resorted to lies: Record a fake podcast, Create fake AI influencers, Create fake testimonials. People are tired of it.

title: Dear AI: You'll never be cooler or funnier than me. Get over it.
content: Is there anything more horrifying than being replaced? I don't want AI writing for me or anyone else. Writing with the assistance of technology isn't just lazy - it's cheating. When it comes to writing, I can't stomach the thought of AI being funnier, wittier or more human than me. David Roth: 'The soul recoils from it.' Maria Bustillos: 'I have yet to see a single paragraph written by AI that didn't gnarl me out with its flatness, woodenness and lack of insight, humour, sensitivity, fun.' Charlie Brooker: 'ChatGPT is just an impersonator. It's mashing up stuff that other people have already done. You still need a human to make it usable.' Words like 'unleapable' and 'hoovering up content' would never be written by AI. Chatbots don't comprehend the joy of breaking the rules. AI needs me more than I need it. What can be more human than vanity?
comments:
Nick Clarke: We're going to be OK.

Chloe Taylor: The change of pace, the wit, just brilliant! I'm yet to see a bot with a good sense of humour.

title: AI Slop or Marketing Gold? Resetting our Relationship with AI in Business - Pascal Fintoni
content: Why become a slave to the tool when you can be its master? You can immediately spot AI-generated content in specialised fields because ironically it often ends up being more complex than human-written content. When you ask AI to 'write me an article about X' without proper guidance, it scrapes existing content and produces something equally lifeless. It's a vicious cycle of mediocrity - the birth of AI slop. 'I never thought I would actually prefer to look at stock photography than an AI-produced image.' Top-performing B2B marketers: understand audience needs vastly superior to competitors, produce less content of higher quality, use AI for efficient workflows not to replace creativity. 'Sloppy preparation gives you AI slop.' Think of AI as your sous-chef.
comments: Jo Long: Love this - and 'AI Slop', what a great term!

title: ChatGPT and the Copycat Chronicles: The Rise of Boring AI Copywriting - Mike Saunders
content: If you're using ChatGPT to write social posts, you'll notice that it uses the word 'thrilled' ALOT to introduce posts on social media. It happens so often it's become an internal joke here at Digitlab. ChatGPT uses 'thrilled' because it sees people using it a lot. ChatGPT is learning to write good copy from masses of people who can't write good copy. George Carlin's famous quote: 'Never underestimate the power of stupid people in large groups.' ChatGPT is a great tool, but it's not a good copywriter; in fact, it's a reasonably terrible one. ChatGPT is a copycat, an imitator who won't bring anything creative or original to the table. 'Thrilled' has become overused and unoriginal, making content less engaging and the writer sound insincere or inauthentic. Good copywriters are a rare breed. They find emotion, shift change, and move people. They use words to mould opinion, create desire, and make our world more interesting. Don't expect AI to speak to your customer's hearts. Only heartbeats connect to other heartbeats.
comments:
Matt Hicks: The middle-of-the-road ChatGPT copy is ubiquitous but increasingly easy to spot.

Charlene Udal: Long live human copywriters that deserve their God-given gift to be used forever.

James Keir: AI is not creative it can only copy/mimic what has already been done. 'Rubbish in = Rubbish out.'

Greg Groenewald: This predictable default to the lowest common denominator means original content will never be on the endangered list.

title: Humans vs Robots: A Comparison of 3 AI Copywriting Services - Nikhil Rughani
content: We reviewed three AI copywriting services: copy.ai, Copy Shark, and Funnel Scripts. Copy.ai: results mostly made sense but were generic and superficial, lacking the flow and personal touch of real human writing. In one case the AI misunderstood the product name entirely. Copy Shark: sentences left unfinished, punctuation inconsistent, content didn't make sense. The content appeared as generic and monotone as copy.ai. What stands out like a giant copywriting elephant is the lack of personalisation. The key to creating good copy isn't so much in the writing - it's actually the hours of research, briefing, and insights into the customer mind. Only once we understand your uniqueness and your clientele do we start to write. Good copy is effective and it converts because it speaks directly to the heart and mind of your ideal audience. Funnel Scripts: $797 price tag, less generic due to question-based briefing system, but still less personalised than a human copywriter who actually takes the time to understand you and your business.
comments:
Tim Entwisle: Letting an IT person design an App to write copy... ha ha ha ha ha!

Jules Dan: If you don't know squat about your audience, don't expect it to write good copy that converts. Gary Halbert talks about 90% of all great copy is in the research. Don't buy into the 'easy button.'

Angela Vithoulkas: Why spend all that time on something that doesn't deliver? It's far better to work with a real writer who gets what you're doing for your business.

title: AI Isn't Going Anywhere - Prerna Malik
content: (Content unavailable - LinkedIn login wall)

title: AI Writing Sucks: 4 Facts No One Is Talking About - Denis Metev
content: AI Just Rephrases: Artificial intelligence is not capable to think on its own, to be creative, to tell a story. What it can do is gather information already published on the web and rephrase it. Sometimes AI doesn't even rephrase, it simply uses entire sentences just as they were written. AI Doesn't Save Time: AI can do its rephrasing in minutes, but you still need a writer or editor to review and make heavy edits so the content is at least tolerable. AI Promotes Laziness And Low Quality: It's so easy to get lazy since AI will write an article in minutes. You start skipping the editing or reviewing part. Then you publish low quality content that no one in their right mind would want to read. AI Can Be Dead Wrong: It's one thing to publish incorrect information about general topics, and a completely different thing when you're publishing about healthcare. AI doesn't understand context too well, and can mix up combinations of words in a very risky way.
comments: Alexander Vladimirov: Machine Learning Language Models still or always will have absence of human randomness. Data sets used for these models are predetermined and that is a result of generating poorly written content.

title: AI-Generated Content Is How You Lose Trust - Ilya Bezdelev
content: AI-generated content is how you lose trust. I wrote a thoughtful comment under a post of someone with 100k+ followers and they responded with a fairly long statement that was very generic and didn't add much to the conversation. The structure goes as follows: Restate the post in the most generic artificial way possible in tone that no humans use. Ask a generic question like 'how could this be improved further?' My pattern-matching brain sees those right away and cringes. When multiple people use the same plugin and comments are very similar, it's obvious the person didn't take any time to write a response. Maybe they didn't even read the post. AI comments especially those that end with a question are disrespectful. Anyone who sends me an AI-generated comment immediately loses trust with me. I'll not respond, not react and remember to not take that person seriously in the future.
comments:
Yiyang Hibner: 'How could this be improved further?' /s. Also great post - it's pretty easy to identify inauthenticity these days.

Stefanos Makris: GenAI has tremendous potential to boost productivity but cannot be viewed as one-size-fits-all. What makes great products and brands is strong differentiation. AI is at a clear disadvantage here - results are expected to conform to previously encountered/trained patterns. Human ingenuity is the ultimate differentiator amongst a sea of mediocre but hyper-optimized commodity solutions.

Nabil R.: Tested the AI comment and surprisingly enough the question at the end came as expected.

Artem Polevoy: I hope that there is common sense that we don't really want to put interpersonal communication on AI.

title: Artificial Language Sweeteners Hijacking AI-Generated Text - Uli Hitzel
content: Did you notice all these fluffy, overused words like 'unravel', 'delve into' and 'intricate' in every other text you read? It's likely that this text is AI-generated. Imagine sitting down to catch up with an old friend and instead of a heartfelt chat, you're bombarded with marketing buzzwords. Author had a conversation with his GPT-4 powered AI buddy Dave about these words. Dave's list of 'jazz-it-up' words: Delve, Unravel, Unleash, Embark, Illuminate, Bolster, Galvanize, Decipher, Foster, Catalyst. Dave: 'They're like the artificial sweetener of language. Slap them on when you want to fake the gourmet experience, but deep down, you know it's just the same old plain words hiding underneath.' If our language models are churning out text loaded with fluffy words, it's because they've been feasting on a diverse array of marketing and PR content from the internet. Has someone tinkered with ChatGPT default settings, turning up the dial to 'cheerful PR chatter'? Use AI to enhance creativity but resist the temptation to rely solely on entire texts generated in seconds along with its artificial sweeteners.
comments:
Thomas J. Smart: It has become a new hobby of mine to DM people who have used unedited GenAI text in their articles and social media posts. Just to make them aware of how obvious it is.

Harald Battran: It's crucial to strike a balance between leveraging technology and retaining the essence of personal or brand identity.

title: AI Slop: The Unseen Flood of AI-Generated Content - Neil Sahota
content: Avoiding AI-generated content online has become nearly impossible. Tech experts coined 'Slop' for content created using generative AI primarily for monetization - low-quality material to flood the internet. The underlying economic model: even a handful of clicks on AI-crafted pages can cover their minimal production costs. AI-generated meal planning app in New Zealand recommended recipes including chlorine gas and glue. On Amazon, AI-authored mushroom foraging books contained dangerous misinformation identifying poisonous mushrooms as edible. Jason Koebler coined 'zombie internet' where bots, inactive accounts, and real users intermingle creating nonsensical online experience. Users increasingly misidentify legitimate ads as AI-generated slop, undermining trust and effectiveness of digital advertising. The biggest industry players are now the ones using AI to generate content.
comments:
Christopher Lowden: How does AI slop differ from the VAST amount of HUMAN SLOP that already exists on the internet? These are value judgements about what is 'GOOD' and 'BAD.'

Naveen Verma: People are using AI to write about subjects they don't have full knowledge about. LinkedIn should make ways to identify AI generated articles and promote original content more.

title: How to (not) Sound Like a Robot: Content Marketing in the Age of AI Writing Tools - Tatum Hindman
content: 59% of marketers already use AI tools to optimize content, but only 13% believe AI-generated content is indistinguishable from human-generated content. AI still writes like a robot pretending to be human. Humans have emotions and can relate to the challenges and feelings of their audience - something even the most sophisticated AI cannot do. Comparison example: AI version lists facts about electric bikes; human version captures feelings of riding one ('wind in your hair without the charley horse in your leg'). AI tends to write long sentences with far too many words or use jargon that adds little value. Ann Handley: 'Writing is a full-body contact sport. You need to participate fully. Your brain. Your hands. Your personality. Your voice. All of it. We can't passively sit back and let AI write for us.'

title: AI Writing from the POV of a Writer Who's Used It for Months - Hunter Somerville
content: AI writing is definitely coming for some jobs. But AI writing is also riding a wave of hype. Being first gets noticed. It doesn't mean the work is great, or has any ROI. It gets attention. Ryan Reynolds used it for an ad - the human-written part was well-written, the AI-written part was ok. If the entire ad were written by AI, you'd just think 'Well that was a mediocre but functional ad.' That's where we are with AI writing. It's functional. It's the fastest working junior writer in the world. Big ideas, nuanced scripts, and human insight-driven writing will likely never be taken over by AI. AI-generated art won't ever resonate the way human-created art does: We love knowing that a human made it. Two identical pieces of art - one AI, one by an artist with an incredible backstory - which will be loved more? We will always want to know/feel the human touch in real creative work.
comments:
Mark Reichard: Trends come and go, great ideas come and stay.

Siya Mali: Is it possible that this distinction will start being less relevant overtime - especially in the ad industry, where you don't need the artist's backstory to create an emotional reaction?

title: AI Prompt Engineers vs. Copywriters - Nick Maciag
content: I keep seeing job postings for 'AI Prompt Engineers.' 6-figure salaries to write for ChatGPT. And I can't help but think... That's just copywriting. We've been doing this for decades. Understanding audience. Tone. Context. Nuance. Knowing which words unlock which emotions. Companies will pay $120k for someone to talk to AI. But they'll lowball copywriters at $50k to create the work AI is trying to replace. They'll invest in the tool. Not the craft. They'll fund the middleman. Not the maker. In a few years, when AI gets better at understanding plain English, those jobs will disappear too. And we'll realize we should've been investing in people the whole time.
comments:
Sky Yang: The best 'prompt engineers' are just people who already understood how to communicate clearly. The real bottleneck was never the tool.

Dave Kline: We're confusing novelty with value. Real skill has always been in knowing what to say, not just where to type it.

Michael Gardon: The real value has always been in understanding people, not just prompting tools.

title: When Everyone Sounds the Same, Originality Wins - Chaya Mathew
content: Scroll through LinkedIn and you'll notice dozens of posts with perfect grammar, impeccable tone, and zero personality. They sound polished but eerily similar, as if we've all been templated. When we copy-paste AI-generated content without a second thought, we risk becoming indistinguishable. AI tools pull from everything that's already out there - it remixes, reframes, and summarizes what exists. The content can feel hollow, incomplete, superficial. Without your unique insight, it lacks the very thing that sets you apart. Consumers perceive AI-written communications as less authentic, potentially impacting brand trust. The rise of AI slop - low-quality AI-generated content flooding the internet - contributes to misinformation and diminishes quality. Think of your voice as a fingerprint - completely unique. AI can help you write, but it can't replicate your experiences, humor, hesitation, or conviction. Originality isn't optional anymore - it's your edge!

title: Why You Shouldn't Trust AI Copywriting Software with Your Content - Monty Montgomery
content: AI copywriting software is as intriguing as it is disappointing. Platforms like Jasper promise 'smarter' work but often spit out content requiring an hour of edits. AI Replicates Rather than Creates: it scrapes the web for relevant content, dilutes it, and scrubs out plagiarism. It requires human articles to write new articles. The difference between human and AI 'stitching' is the latter has little consideration for voice and tone. They may offer formal/casual/funny tone options but rarely include pop-culture references, catchy metaphors, and popular idioms. Robot writers haven't nailed down the emotional aspect of writing - the most important standard to hit. Jasper costs $82/month for Boss Mode - if you'd rather not fix a robot's mistakes for an hour, pass on these services. Less Than 20% of Companies Employ AI Copywriting Software.
comments: Julie Ann Howlett: Thanks for advocating for us freelance writers! The robots can't get us down.

title: Can You Tell If Something's Written by ChatGPT? - Rowan Drury
content: Can you tell if something's written by/with ChatGPT? I'm starting to think so. ChatGPT is another tool in my writer's toolbox - useful, but just like a hammer, it can't do much without the hand that swings it. ChatGPT has this formulaic style that only the most specific prompts can steer it away from. Flowery adjectives bolster cliches in sentences that don't fit the context and try to hide their irrelevance behind a self-important delivery. The biggest giveaway of all? The word 'seamless' - this AI assistant loves it. Will this generated style shift our language to become homogeneous, where every company, website or service sounds like an over-excited thesaurus?
comments:
Florencia Linarez: 'Over-excited thesaurus' really put into words what I have been thinking.

Carolina Silvandersson: All text wrapped in fancy wording and empty words using 'intriguing', 'astonishing', 'delves', 'explore', 'enlightened' - that's ChatGPT! You feel the AI in the text!

Wu-chiao Huang: I've never seen so many 'hone's in my entire life. Suddenly everyone's using it.

Romilly Golding: I'm surprised 'delve' isn't in there, or 'embark on a journey.'

Astrid von Schmeling: My AI friend seems to love the word 'surge'.

Rebecca Oliver: I'm worried that ChatGPT will make us suspicious and even allergic to the gorgeous words that our language has to offer.

title: Is SEO and AI Destroying the Art of Copywriting? - Robyn Edwards
content: Content is changing. Does anyone else find themselves adding *Reddit* or *Quora* to their Google search bar to get a human answer? What used to be a 30-second research job now takes at least five minutes. I've lost count of the number of times I have read an article that doesn't make any sense - but the well-placed keywords have put them at the top of Google. An increasing number of 'copywriting positions' are editing AI content to 'make it seem more human.' The internet no longer needs perfect content, just 'ok' content with the right keywords. The pandemic saturated the market, driving prices down. Why pay someone $75/hour when you can hire someone elsewhere for $1 per 100 words? An editor looking to build a portfolio will pick that rewrite up for $5 on Fiverr. Google has proved products and purchases are more important than information.
comments:
Jamieson Lee Hill: Focus on human-centred creative niches. Chat GPT just churns out cliches but videogames require innovative ideas and thinking out of the box.

Sam O'Neil: I see so many awful articles that read more like repetitive Onion pieces that restate the idea from the headline a dozen different ways instead of expanding to teach something useful.

title: Using AI? Don't Lose Your (Brand) Voice - Superkick Branding
content: AI is great for streamlining marketing and content creation processes. Just make sure you're not losing your brand voice. If you get lazy, you're risking putting out generic content that strays from your brand identity. Think of AI content creation like picking out an anniversary card - you don't want to grab the first card you see without reading it. The message might be bland, irrelevant, or something you would never say. That's a surefire way to spend your anniversary sleeping on the couch. AI isn't a cheat code. It's a powerful tool but one that still requires a human touch, especially when it comes to your brand voice. Develop a Brand Voice Guide. Train AI Tools to Recognize Your Brand Voice. Monitor AI-Generated Content. Regularly Update Your Brand Voice Guide.

title: The Limitations of AI as a Copywriting Tool (as written by ChatGPT) - James Daniel
content: The most interesting thing I've ever read by ChatGPT is a self-penned admission of its own inadequacy. ChatGPT wrote: AI has no understanding of the content it regurgitates. It can mimic human language and produce coherent sentences, but lacks comprehension, context, and ability to truly grasp the essence of what it's writing. AI tends to regurgitate cliches and recycle tired phrases - an abundance of unoriginal, uninspiring copy. AI remains emotionless and incapable of understanding human emotions. AI-generated copy comes across as sterile, detached. The human touch sets great copy apart from mediocre. Author's take: 'Why in the name of Satan's buttocks would you use ChatGPT? Next time you need brain surgery, don't hire an expensive brain surgeon. I'll do it for nothing with a rusty scalpel I found round the back of Nando's.' When even ChatGPT admits it's a terrible writer, you'd have to be stark raving bananas to let it loose on your copy.
comments:
Jenni Davies: ChatGPT is currently a bit shit, yes. But if anything has the ability to rapidly evolve, it's AI. There will be no better combination than a passionate creative using ChatGPT.

Abigail Stavroulakis-McMahon: AI generated copy is an incredible tool in the hands of a creative and strategic copywriter. But an absolute let down by itself.

Dave Bull: 'Tends to regurgitate cliches and recycle tired phrases' sounds like some humans we know of.

title: Jasper AI Review - Jussi Hyvarinen
content: (Page no longer exists - 404)

title: The Human Touch in Copywriting: Why It's Irreplaceable in an AI-Driven World - Anca Nicula
content: AI can churn out words at record speeds, predict trends, and even mimic iconic tones. But where do you fit in as a writer? The essence of compelling copywriting lies in the nuances that only you can truly capture. AI can analyze trends, structure sentences, and make data-based suggestions, but it doesn't feel. It doesn't understand why someone might buy a book because they're feeling lonely or how a single message can brighten someone's day. Your empathy, life experiences, and unique ability to understand others make your words impactful. Think about your own story - personal struggles, unforgettable travels, heartbreak. These moments shape how you communicate, giving your writing a depth that no algorithm could replicate. In a world of machines, your creativity and human touch are irreplaceable.

title: The Rise of AI Slop: What is it and Why it Matters - Neil Gentleman-Hobbs
content: AI slop has emerged as a derogatory term for the flood of low-quality, AI-generated content polluting the digital landscape. Driven primarily by profit motives, this slop manifests in various forms, from text and images to videos and even entire websites. Think of it as the digital equivalent of junk food -- superficially appealing but ultimately unsatisfying and potentially harmful. Common forms: Vague and Buzzword-Laden Text filled with trendy keywords but lacking insight; Low-Effort Memes relying on shock value; Questionable News Articles disseminating misinformation; Bizarre AI-Generated Images like the infamous Shrimp Jesus; Inappropriate or Misleading Ads; Fake Books with fake authors and plagiarized text. Problems: It clogs digital feeds wasting time, deceives advertisers into spending on platforms with little real engagement, misleads consumers, and harms creatives who are having their IP stolen under fair use.
comments: Neil Gentleman-Hobbs: Currently the tech bro cartel appear to be favouring the orange theme.

title: ChatGPT Loves a Meaningless Tapestry - Jason Zabel
content: ChatGPT LOVES a meaningless tapestry or mosaic metaphor, even when I tell it to never ever say these words again. I am afraid this is just another sad example of a company that is in the pocket of Big Tapestry.
comments:
Ashleigh Penrod: I had been asking ChatGPT for some early brainstorming help with something and it kept coming back with compound words, so I asked it to please stop with those and it was like, "Certainly! I understand. How about HouseLight or BrightNight? HouseNight?" Big Tapestry strikes again.
Ilana Horwitz: You are not a fan of big tapestry??

title: Obvious AI Words You Must Avoid In Your Writing - Echez O Ford
content: I recently got a big job to edit and update a website entire content. Before I used any plagiarism tool to run the site audit, I could already tell that it was saturated with AI content. The site was losing rank. And ROI was flying out the window. If you are lazy and allow AI to do all of your work, you will come across as robotic. Your content will not get to the heart of your audience. 20 common AI words to avoid: Amazing, Disruptive, Remarkable, Breakthrough, Paving the way, State-of-the-art, The rapid pace of development, Unprecedented, Revolutionary, Cutting-edge, Innovative, Excitingly, Only time will tell, Revolutionize the way, Significantly enhances, Groundbreaking advancement, Bringing us one step closer, Transformative power, Push the boundaries, Next-generation, Remember. Instead of revolutionize the way, say improve the way. Instead of cutting-edge, say advanced or modern. Write the way you talk.
comments:
Benjamin Dinkelmann: The biggest red flag is using delve. This word was rarely used for the longest time, but when ChatGPT came out, its usage exploded. I wonder if AI will homogenize our writing?
Virginia Castro: When reviewing scripts produced by AI, ask yourself: Would I talk like this to a senior VP? Would I explain this topic this when talking to a friend or colleague? Be critical when you review the AI output.
Federico Arbelaez: Crucial Vital Essential Critical Key Top-notch Enable Tailored
Echez O Ford: I just discovered these more AI words: Weaving, Tapestry. This will be added to the list.

title: Words That Make It Obvious Your Text Is Written by AI - Shivangi Jain
content: (Page no longer exists - 404)

title: Do We Care What It Is Like To Read AI-generated Content? - Clark Boyd
content: The most effective examples of GPT-3 content I have seen would fall into this category: Content that no-one wants to write, and no-one wants to read. In other words, the majority of content that is published online today. Much of the content we create is already for machines. Why should it not be written by them too? The impact of generative AI could be an acceleration of the sensory deprivation we already feel today. SEO content is stunningly consistent in its delivery, whether it appears on behalf of Burberry or Barclays. For many writers, any notion of idiosyncratic sensibility is toast once it meets the tyrannical Yoast WordPress plug-in. The reading experience is not the foremost concern here. Publishers care about communicating with Google. An AI could learn to mimic any style, but could it develop its own? That is the key question. The most difficult task for generative AI will be to replicate content that is by people and for people. We have simplified the task for AI by stooping to the machines level for too long. Instead of asking whether AI can churn out enough mediocre content to replace copywriters, we must ask how it can help us to create content that connects with readers on a deeper, human level.
comments:
Fyodor Minakov: This is one heck of an article. Reading this, I could only think -- NOT AI-generated. How do I know for sure? Because I read the entire thing. Content that engages actually matters.
Elena Kuznetsova: I personally can tell when it is AI written. But if it is that easily confused with human writing, there is definitely a need for deeper connection between the reader and the author. It is not enough to be just a good content creator anymore.

title: The Rise of AI Slop and How to Guard Against It - Hawke Media
content: AI slop refers to low-quality, repetitive, and often nonsensical content generated en masse by artificial intelligence tools with minimal human oversight. It lacks originality -- AI models rely on existing data, often regurgitating slightly tweaked versions of widely available information. It has no clear point of view -- unlike human writers who inject experience, insight, and authority, AI-generated slop tends to be generic and lifeless. It is riddled with misinformation. It overwhelms search engines. Risks for brands: Damaging trust and credibility -- customers can spot AI-generated fluff from a mile away. SEO penalties -- Google is actively refining its algorithms to detect and demote low-value AI content. Missed opportunities for thought leadership. Solutions: Use AI as an assistant not a replacement, prioritize human editorial oversight, infuse content with unique insights and experience, monitor performance metrics. A HubSpot study found that while AI-assisted content saw a 20% increase in efficiency, purely AI-generated content had 35% lower engagement than human-led articles.

title: Comparing AI Content Writers: Jasper AI, Writesonic, Copy.ai, Frase.io, and Scalenut - Sana Uqaili
content: Comparison of AI content writers. Jasper AI: $29/month for 20,000 words, 50+ templates including AIDA and PAS frameworks, integrates with SurferSEO, requires significant editing for high quality. Writesonic: $13/month for 47,500 words, cheapest option, needs substantial editing. Copy.ai: $35/month for 40,000 words, high-quality content quickly, built-in plagiarism checker. Frase.io: $44.99/month for 4,000 queries, specializes in content optimization, least editing required. Scalenut: $19/month for 100,000 words, cost-effective for high-volume but requires significant editing. Summary: Frase.io and Copy.ai produce highest quality. Writesonic cheapest. Jasper AI and Frase.io best SEO. Scalenut prioritizes quantity over quality.

title: AI Is a Waste of Time - Maya Mikhailov
content: What if GenAI is all just the latest time suck? Perhaps Generative AI has spread so quickly, not because it is saving us time, but because it is very good at wasting our time. Sure, it might add to the GDP eventually, but making all those fun Midjourney images and Drake mash-ups will also compete with the mind space we usually spend at work. But what if this is okay? So what if we waste a lot of time but discover some new insights and ideas along the way? So AI might be a productivity enhancer and distractor at the same time... not unlike the phone in our hand.
comments:
Marijn Markus: Perhaps generated content will just replace the reshared content we usually entertain ourselves with.
Andreas Welsch: Love the refreshing perspective of what if it is just there vs. the polarizing coverage. Re: Wasting time -- Just wait until GenAI starts powering the next generation of entertainment and experiences.

title: Humans Prefer AI-Generated Copy, Survey Finds - Tero Heinonen
content: This recent study explores whether people prefer content written by humans or AI. Shockingly, according to the article, AI crushes human copywriters already today. Now, when AI has reached near parity, the real differentiation is not anymore the quality of writing, but ability to do research. AI can perform a detailed research of the recipients and context in seconds. The same effort would take hours to days of human work. But marketers job description will change fast. From implementation of details to planning and supervising experiments.
comments:
Max Winter: Is that the best post any human writer can possibly produce? Or just an average one? Because the AI post IS the best the AI can possibly produce. Hyper-personalization is certainly more engaging, but can also get very creepy very quickly.
Davor Magdic: A survey by a SEO company producing automated garbage says consumers prefer automated garbage? Color me surprised.
Mike Betts: The methodology is not completely neutral. Probably 100% human-generated short-form performance-focused content will not survive beyond the next 12 months.
Paul Burchard: Key phrase: while AI won on preference in this survey, that may not translate to actual performance.
Larry Burns: Perhaps because most of the country reads at a 4th grade level?
Michael Webster: Sorry, this nonsense. And you can tell your GPT I said so.

title: Tapestry This and Tapestry That - Brendon Beebe
content: Tapestry this and Tapestry that why does ChatGPT love those words? What else is 100% obvious AI was used?
comments: Trevor Hardy: Revolutionize.

title: AI Fatigue: When Innovation Feels Like Overload - Neil Sahota
content: AI fatigue refers to the mental and emotional exhaustion individuals and organizations experience due to the constant barrage of information, sales pitches, and lofty AI technology promises. This fatigue is compounded by the rapid growth of AI, constant interaction with these technologies, and the increasing pressure to do more with fewer resources. Causes: Constant technological change, unrealistic expectations, lack of understanding. ChatGPT attracted one million members in five days after launching, which took Netflix five years. It now has over 200 million people actively using it every week. Employee engagement is down, with just 43% eager to accept change now, compared to 74% in 2016. Symptoms: cognitive overload, moral and emotional dilemmas, burnout in AI development, job concerns, information overload. Solutions: Focus on the problem not the technology, adopt a thoughtful approach, set achievable goals, build a culture ready for change.
comments:
Leonard D. Lane: The key is solving real problems that align with and support your strategy.
Vijay Chowdhary: Does AI fatigue signal a need for regulation?

title: The Rise of AI Slop: The Future of Content, Attention, and Culture - Sujith Kumar
content: AI slop describes AI-generated content created in large volumes with a focus on quantity rather than quality. Recent reports suggest that 20-50% of the internet may already be AI slop. What is the future of human-made, thoughtful, original content in a world that rewards low-effort AI output with better reach and stronger monetary incentives? AI slop succeeds over traditional content by keeping things simple while embedding uncontrolled elements of uncertainty. It is similar to McDonalds Happy Meal -- simple, low-effort fast food packaged with toys that introduce surprise. Average human attention span dropped from 2.5 minutes in 2004 to 47 seconds in 2019 due to increased exposure to digital tools and social media. AI slop provides content creators and audiences the best of both worlds -- content that is easy to create and easy to consume. An instigation of a quiet rebellion against a world that had historically rewarded elitism, effort and skill over communication and democratization of art.
comments: Michael Voll: AI is fake, lazy, uninspiring, void of integrity or originality, a thief in many cases, and dehumanizing.

title: AI Homogenization Risk - Anna Naveed
content: As companies adopt the same foundational models, the same copilots, and the same AI driven workflows, their organizations begin to behave more similarly. Strategies converge. Outputs converge. Even the mistakes converge. A researcher at the Oxford Internet Institute warned: We risk a world where every company thinks with the same brain. More than sixty percent of large enterprises now standardize critical decisions on a small set of model families. A professor at Stanford: AI does not copy your competitors. It copies the world. And if everyone uses the same model, they copy the same world in the same way. Corporate originality was once shaped by unique internal cultures, leadership philosophies, and analytical frameworks. Now it is shaped by prompt engineering and default presets. The risk is not automation. It is assimilation. As employees rely on the same copilots to draft, write, analyze, and think, their internal cognitive diversity shrinks. A senior behavioral economist: You cannot have a creative culture when everyone uses the same cognitive crutch. AI may become the first technology to erase managerial geography. The danger is not that AI will think for us, but that it will make us forget how to think differently.
comments: Michael Zroback: An excellent analysis marred by an ineffective solution. HR is not influential enough to do anything about the negative effects of AI on an organization.

title: How AI Can Clone Us: A Personal AI Writing Experience - Ashleigh-Ann Mowatt
content: I made a joke with a family member a few days ago that AI could clone us. After publishing this prompt and reading what ChatGPT generated, I am no longer smiling. The Prompt: Describe me based on all our chats -- make it catchy! ChatGPT generated a detailed personal description based on all interactions. While this response is encouraging, it has also made me a bit more mindful of how I use this tool. AI studies you. That is the point. It studies your patterns, your tone, your voice, just like the technology Google uses to dominate the search engine space. So, the more information you feed it, the better the results.
comments: Dianne Jobity: ChatGPT is actively learning about each user. I noticed sometime ago changes in its feedback as it learned my writing style. I also just noticed today that there is a customization feature that you can tell the app more about you... nah I am good. I am not putting out more information than I need to.

title: 9 Common AI Content Creation Mistakes to Avoid - Shabbir Ahmad
content: 9 common AI content creation mistakes: 1. Overdependence on AI -- AI tools are designed to augment, not replace, human creativity. Relying solely on AI leads to uninspired and repetitive content. 2. Ignoring Audience Targeting -- AI may not align with your audience interests and needs. 3. Neglecting Brand Voice -- AI-generated content often lacks consistency, leading to a disjointed brand narrative. 4. Overlooking SEO Optimization -- overdoing it results in keyword stuffing. 5. Failing to Fact-Check -- AI tools may include incorrect or outdated data. 6. Disregarding Content Structure -- AI might struggle with well-structured long-form content. 7. Underestimating Creativity Needs -- AI lacks ability to be genuinely creative or understand nuanced human experiences. 8. Neglecting Legal and Ethical Standards. 9. Overlooking Audience Engagement -- AI-generated content might not resonate on an emotional level.

title: How To Correct The Downside Of AI Content Creation - Shauna Van Mourik
content: Pros of AI content: Increased efficiency, cost-effective, improved consistency, data-driven insights. Cons: Lack of human touch -- AI may lack emotional appeal and creativity of human-generated content, potentially reducing engagement. Limited understanding of tone and context -- may result in inappropriate or insensitive content. Dependence on algorithms. Vulnerability to misinformation. How to correct downsides: Quality control, human oversight, human-AI collaboration, monitor for diversity and bias, avoid plagiarism, maintain relevance, tell YOUR story -- no AI tool can speak to your unique experience so take the time to create bespoke content that speaks to you, your journey and what it means to do what you do as only you can do it. P.S. this article was made from bits and pieces of AI -- could you tell?

title: AI Copywriting: The Future or Still a Work in Progress? - Nspiresoft
content: AI copywriting allows marketers to create content faster and more efficiently -- generate copy in minutes rather than hours or days. Benefits: faster production, improved quality through data analysis, reduced costs. Limitations: AI-generated content can lack creativity and originality -- may not produce emotionally resonant or thought-provoking content. Lacks the human touch -- struggles to capture nuances of human emotion, humor, or tone. May not be suitable for long-form content like whitepapers or case studies. The role of human copywriters is unlikely to disappear completely. In the future, human copywriters and AI-powered tools will work together, with AI helping to streamline and humans providing the essential human touch.

title: The AI Shift: Copywriting New Landscape - Martin Jeffrey
content: Some agencies have made the shortsighted decision to replace copywriters with AI, banking on mass-produced content to boost their digital strategies. This fails to recognize the unique strengths human copywriters bring. AI is a tool, not a replacement, and it comes with limitations. There are still aspects of writing that cannot be automated: capacity for human empathy, creativity, humour, and comprehension of nuanced cultural references. AI-written copy presents opportunity for copywriters to transition into roles as fact-checkers -- AI makes mistakes, especially with complex, nuanced subjects or rapidly changing information. The move towards niche writing is another notable trend -- while AI is competent at generating generic copy, it struggles with specialised, industry-specific content. The relationship between AI and copywriters will likely be characterised more by collaboration than competition. AI can do many things, but it cannot replace the creativity, empathy, and unique perspectives that human writers bring.
